title: 2025 12 02 - HE (Part 1)
displayTitle: 2025-12-01 - Health Committee - 2024/25 Annual review of the Health Quality and Safety Commission New Zealand [Part 1]
topic: 2024/25 Annual review of the Health Quality and Safety Commission New Zealand
description: Tuesday 2 December 2025 | Health Committee 2024/25 Annual review of the Health Quality and Safety Commission New Zealand Hearing of evidence: Health Quality and Safety Commission Professor Sunny Collings, Chief Executive Rae Lamb, Board Chair Richard Hamblin, Director of Health Quality Intelligence Don Matheson, Director, National Mortality Review Committee Carlton Irving, Director of Māori Health and Consumer
descriptionSummary: 2024/25 Annual review of the Health Quality and Safety Commission New Zealand. Health Quality and Safety Commission. Professor Sunny Collings, Chief Executive

chunks.text: Good morning. Good morning. I saw you waving patiently outside for while. Yes, we knew as long as you were in the care, we were probably safe.

Good. Good to have you back here. Um, for the committee, thank you for making yourself available. We have you here for an hour, so, uh, I'll pass it to you for opening comments and then we'll open it up to me because Got it. Oh, Kiara. Um, and I'll keep my opening comments very short. I have read the instructions and I know that we've seen you a couple of times this year anyways, so you're familiar with us.

But, um, uh, as you know, I'm Ray Lamb, I'm chair of the Health Quality and Safety Commission. Um, I'm delighted to have with me today Professor Sunny Collins, who's our new Chief executive since August. Um, Sonny has taken over from Dr. Peter Janssen, who had to leave us in February due to ill health. Um, as I said, I'm not gonna spend a lot of time explaining what we do.

You've heard from us a couple of times this year already, but I would like to note a couple of things and also highlight a couple of things 'cause we're pretty proud of some of the work that's represented in our annual report this year. Um, a key function of ours, of course, is leading and coordinating work across the sector to monitor and improve the quality and safety of services. Uh, we've got a legislative mandate as a voice for consumers and we hold significant expertise with data information and use that to understand what's happening in the

system and how to improve it. What we see and say is informed not just by system data, but also from patients themselves. And we think that's pretty important. Um, and when we see issues, we act quickly to escalate these 'cause of course, we're a couple of steps removed from the front line. We can't necessarily do it ourselves. As with many organizations, we've undergone significant change in recent years to ensure that we can deliver in our priorities within our resourcing.

Healthcare. Acquired harm can and does have serious consequences. Oh yeah. So avoiding these harms, of course, leads to longer, healthier lives and also reduced costs through avoiding unnecessary remedial work. We believe our role is more important than ever given the change, current challenges and changes across the system. But as I said before, we can't do it alone.

We have to work really closely with other agencies to lead and influence change. And we're constantly trying to be better at showing not just what we do, but what we achieve. And this committee gave us a good reminder of the need to do that last time we saw you. So please be assured that we are working on it. Um, we really want to demonstrate what we do, um, how it makes a difference, uh, to the health system for those who work in it, and also most particularly those who use the system.

Hopefully you can see that in this year's annual report. It's always difficult, um, when your work is a few steps removed from the front line to actually demonstrate the impact that you have when there's a few other players between what we do and, and what happens out there, um, to, it's hard, difficult to clearly identify sometimes what has been achieved for the end users, which is the consumers, um, patients themselves.

We are very proud of our latest annual report and the work it represents from the last year, as you will have seen it notes many achievements. Uh, and I just wanna highlight a couple, uh, including hearing from over 200,000 people through our patient experience surveys. So these help to highlight what's working well and what the challenges are, and they inform the insights and the information that we provide to decision makers and service providers they can lead to and allow us to monitor better patient experiences.

And we think that, you know, some of this work over the last 10 years, for instance, has lead to the fact that we now have consistently significantly higher rates of people saying that they feel involved in decisions around their care and discussions around their care. The production and dissemination of insights, reports, alerts, data intelligence, and other information on the quality and safety about health services enables early identification and escalation of issues and risks.

And it supports evidence-based responses. So an example of that is the work that we are doing with Health New Zealand and a CC to try and address the ongoing issues with pressure injuries outside of hospital. Mm-hmm. A new atlas of healthcare variation domain on COPD. So that's chronic obstructive pulmonary disease highlights both regional and demographic differences in prevalence, hospitalization and medication use.

The reason I mention it, the reason it's important and the ATLAS itself is important is that we have data that shows that clinicians respond to what's in that atlas around the variation that we see and they change their practice. So when we updated the ATLAS recently, we found, for instance, that 40,000 more diabetes patients are receiving appropriate medication than five years ago, and potentially preventable hospitalizations have dropped 15%, uh, in mortality review.

Um, there's been a fair bit of change and a lot of work going on. One of the things I wanted to highlight is that we've increased the level and of analysis and improved the institutional positioning of perioperative mortality in particular. So two, two tools that we developed are helping Health New Zealand to identify changes in surgical mortality outcomes.

And we've assisted Health New Zealand to set up a group within their national clinical governance group to look at any variance and to take necessary actions to address it. And that's a pretty important initiative. It's actually seeing some action being done much more, um, immediately within the organization itself to address some of the things that have been highlighted.

Finally, I'd like to highlight the framework for clinical governance. We developed, it's now being used across the sector to strengthen accountability and improve patient care and experience. And again, we've been working and continue to work closely with Health, health New Zealand on that and also to ensure that their model for clinical governance in the regions is aligned with it. The new early warning system for aged residential care we've, we've delivered is enabling faster identification and treatment of acutely unwell aged care residents.

It's the first of its kind internationally, so we are pretty proud of it. Um, and there's very high interest across the whole aged care sector, residential aged care sector, enrolling it out, implementing it. We're working on digital tools and other things to help them with that. And finally, we've also, with others, made significant progress and trauma care via the National Trauma Care Trauma Program, including improved detection of brain injuries through consistent use of post-traumatic and assessments.

So, um, those are just a few of the things I wanted to highlight. As I said, we, we know that it's a work in progress. We're trying to get better at demonstrating our impact, what we achieve. Uh, we're pretty pleased with this year's annual report. We think it shows a good year and we welcome your questions. Excellent. Thank you. We have two questions. Uh, Andrew, then Asha and then Ricardo.

Thank you. I may, Mr. Chair just a primary and, and three or four subs. Um, thank you for all you do. The questions I'm going to ask you are, are going to go to a number of organizations 'cause we've got claims from the mental health minister in particular that we are seeing increased treatment access, increased training, more community workers, more frontline workers that people have got somewhere to go.

I'm wanting to know why there isn't a national mental health safety dashboard. And the 300 indicators that you have provided in your report. Um, as you know, our work in terms of the, um, mental health and addiction quality improvement, um, program has been taken back into Health New Zealand.

So our main window into what's happening in the mental health area is through the requirement now to always report and review seclusion. Um, and then through our more general work. But I could, I'll ask Richard Hamlin 'cause he'll be a bit more aware of what, of any work's been done in terms of your dashboard. Actual dashboard question if you introduce yourself, Richard.

So I'm Richard Hamlin, director of Health Quality Intelligence at the commission. Um, so we've not, uh, really advanced a mental health dashboard ourselves largely 'cause others were doing it and, um, particularly the Mental Health and Wellbeing Commission, uh, did a lot of work about their own assessment frameworks and, um, there are other things we can do, uh, that we have to do and we were happy to let them take the lead on that.

Uh, there are things that are emerging though, one of which is a collaboration, uh, with, uh, the commission around a, uh, patient experience survey for those who are in, uh, mental health, uh, or have experienced mental health services. We also have, uh, produced data from the primary care survey, uh, which we put inside an atlas showing mental health, um, the experience of mental health care, uh, amongst that cohort patients.

So we have done things, but we've, uh, allowed the Mental Health and Wellbeing Commission, which has primacy to take the lead Here. Thank you. And in your, um, leader of expectation or statement of intent, it says that you must and report in on areas of increased risk. We have seen from, uh, crisis performance and it's been deteriorating.

There's abandoned calls, longer waits, Maori over representation, police cells, crisis assessments and so on. Why have you never issued a quality alert on crisis service safety? Do we, do we get that data? It's not data we don't actually have access to. So our alerts tend to be those that we, um, automatically have.

Why don't you get access to it? I mean, when I read the paperwork, it looks like there is a, an umbrella, uh, requirement and mental health seems to be completely missing from the data that you are providing insights into. I wouldn't say completely missing, but it's certainly less of a priority, largely because of the existence of the, the Mental Health and Wellbeing Commission.

But it's a, it's a fair challenge to raise. Um, we've also got the same regarding Ed Mental health harm in, in, in ed Wait Times and emergency departments. Have you done any, um, analysis into that? Uh, not specifically on mental health, but on ed Wait, times more generally? We've done some work which we have shared with.

And finally, Sorry. So just, just following on that, that line of question and, um, a lot of the answers do seem to be that it's the Mental Health and Wellbeing Commission is leading this. H how, how do you two work together, earn something? Well, a good example would actually be the, the specific thing we're doing now on, um, um, a patient experience survey, uh, for mental health, um, uh, for mental health patients or for mental health code people use mental health services.

Uh, there, it's a matter of early engagement with them about the design, uh, involving them in our, uh, design processes, sharing the data. We provide them with data to support, uh, their, uh, annual reports and so forth. So there's a flow of communication between us, uh, and we're, we are, you know, we can work together. We do. Mm-hmm.

So just, I, I can just add at that we also meet at a leadership level too, quite regularly, um, with the chair of the board and also, um, the chief executive. And I might say just a slight declaration here, both Sunny and I, I've just recently been appointed to the mental health commission board, so both Sunny and I are on that board. So there are opportunities for greater cohesion across the two.

You've got one more? One more. So what, what do You see is, um, what do you see as your kind of responsibility in this partnership? Uh, to support, to provide, uh, support where our expertise is there and to work with them where they have expertise that we can use and things that are very clearly our responsibility, like the patient experience, um, program.

So we would also see, um, some, I think mental health at, at the sharp end through the SAC one and SAC two adverse event reporting as well. So we, I guess we see we're going to see increasingly patient experience, which we can bring into the mix working with the commission. And at the other end we see the adverse event reporting, which obviously we work straight back through Health New Zealand providers on in particular. Ingrid.

Thank you. You did mention seclusion, um, and there's a le level of granularity in some of your other markers such as falls, infections and medication harm. But when it comes to seclusion, we don't see anything around inpatient sec exclusion, inpatient restraint, bed occupancy pressure, overcrowding risk, or, uh, sentinel events in acute mental health units.

So why are you not including that level, uh, of detail in your mental health, uh, national safety marker program? Well, this was, uh, designed with the system about the things that were most useful to them. Uh, I'm not actually sure there's a huge amount more detail than we'd give in, um, the mental health seclusion than you would find in our other quality and safety markers.

Those were very much designed, uh, with one outcome measure and a series of process measures. Um, for the mental health, uh, seclusion program, the process measures were quite disparate as, 'cause people were doing different things in different places to get things down. And there was a, a single measure or a di a a diverse way of reporting a single measure as an outcome, which is pretty common to what we would do.

Given everything you've said and where you say the accountabilities lie, would it be a fair assessment to say that your picture of the mental health system based on the data or lack of data that you have and the lack of analysis is probably, uh, very thin and that, that this lies elsewhere? I, I think we see part of it, and I think it, it comes back to, in fact, how we work with a lot of things.

We, we, we are one of, we get different pieces of information in different parts of the system and it's about being able to kind of do well at putting it together. But I think you're right, we do see parts of it. We don't see the completeness. Um, and I think there is opportunity to do more in that area, particularly working more closely with the mental health Commission. Not, not many parts though compared to other Thanks, Mr. Chair.

And, um, noting that we've had many opportunities to talk. I just wanna go a bit deeper into some of the areas in the annual report. Thanks. I'd like to ask you some questions about figure two please, which, um, looks like a really interesting effort to try and quantify the, um, value of your, of your work.

Um, can can you please explain how these, um, uh, savings are calculated? And what I'm trying to understand is, is this a real readout on what's happening now or an estimate projecting benefits based on the evidence about the interventions that you use?

I'm gonna ask Richard, because he's much better than I'm in explaining things like, and how, how we come to these calculations. Okay. So there's two, there's two elements here. Um, the first is the avoided cost of harm. The second is the, uh, value of avoided disability. Just life here is lost. You're comfortable with the concept.

So, um, on the avoided cost of harm, when we started any of these programs designed to, uh, change people's be oh, change behavior in the system so that you've got better outcomes. And there was always a clear evidential link between them. We also looked for the best evidence we could find on the, an estimate of cost.

Uh, so this is all from the peer review literature. We will take an estimate of cost. We have applied that, uh, throughout. We have not adjusted for inflation. The way that we use this is we, uh, the way that we have measured our outcomes is we take the specific outcome harm. We measured it over a baseline period prior to the introduction of the program, uh, classic quality improvement style.

We then monitored what happened to that outcome as we saw the change in, in, um, process. Um, once we had seen a statistically significant reduction, uh, um, measured by statistical process control methods, uh, we then reset to a new level of, um, current harm.

The difference between those two was used to estimate or to calculate actually a, uh, a reduction in harm based on what would've occurred had the same initial level of harm being Applied patients. Okay. You get the prize for the best answer at annual review. Thank you. So the, I mean, that's great and it's good to see any effort to calculate the Yeah.

Uh, from a small number of your programs. The, um, the, uh, benefit, uh, our party is very interested in the opportunities to prevent more harm in the health system because of this. You know, not only do people get out of hospital well, but there's also these, uh, um, opportunities to save money and bed nights, um, through that you've outlined here.

Uh, what are the other opportunities to do this that you currently don't have programs on? If I might suggest, one of the most interesting ones to me is pressure injury. Um, which has increased particularly, uh, both in and outside of hospital, particularly in the years, uh, following, uh, the COVID uh, experience.

Um, what is quite interesting is about 10 years ago, some early work, uh, was undertaken between ourselves a, c, C, um, the then district health boards and others around what would be necessary to, um, address what the issue then, because I mean, obviously occur and bad things when they do.

Um, my sense is this is a much more complex one to address. 'cause there's a lot more going on, uh, in some ways actually rather like the, the seclusion. Um, and it needs more people to come to the, the party to do things. I mean, what's actually quite interesting about most of the ones we report here, there was pretty clear cause and effect I think with some of these other ones, pressure injuries.

It's more there. But it does seem to me what we are seeing there is a definite opportunity. It's also been a persistent problem. So we are seeing it again and again in each set of data that comes through that it's not kind of shifting. That's correct, isn't it, Richard? Yeah. And in fact, in fact, it's, it, it, as I say, it had worsened and it is stuck it and kind of new normal level, probably 20 to 30% higher than it was prior.

The other area that we've seen, and this is highlighted through some existing programs, but has come up more recently and certainly, um, uh, struck us, um, at, in terms of some of the insights reporting is, and bearing in mind the aging population is the, um, care pathways is the, the difficulties that are arising, which you can't just put down to the fact that there are more older people and increasingly more older people, but where, you know, older people are sort of more older people are spending longer in hospital

and there aren't those kind of step down. There's not that continuity that we need to be really thinking about between primary care, hospital aged care, you know, other community supports. Just two more, Mr. Chair. Um, what are the types of, um, solutions that might be able to be implemented for the pressure sore area?

And I, what I'm trying to pressure injury, you put it, uh, um, is this a matter, um, of simple checklists like you use in some of these other areas? Or is this about staffing in a, in a and resourcing of aged care? It's more complex than that, isn't it? Uh, uh, yes, yes to both.

And, you know, there's, you know, there are, uh, easy access to appropriate mattresses, for example, would be a another example. Um, there is probably something about home care as well, not just age residential care and sort of, uh, uh, those sorts of things. There's probably something around, um, I was gonna say health literacy, you know, sort of.

So I mean, it feels to me this is a very most multifactorial approach. My 0.15. Wonderful. Um, we've thank you for those comments about pressure injuries. Just interested, if any, um, uh, other officials want to broaden out that comment to other areas where there might be opportunities over and above pressure injuries.

We shouldn't let the opportunity to do good be passed up. Did you want to say in terms of mortality review, DERs there, I mean we, one of the things with mortality review is, you know, we have to prioritize and do what we can. Um, but if we had the opportunity, where would we see it In terms of wiping it?

Uh, Don Matheson's my name. I'm the director of mortality reviews and thanks. Uh, oh, there's, there's huge opportunities and mortality reviews. There's between eight and 10,000 preventable deaths every year. Uh, probably the tricky thing for us though is which, which of those does our role in health quality and safety can make a difference?

Um, uh, and of course there's lots of other parts of the sector also focused on reducing those voidable deaths from a CC and, um, uh, uh, uh, MSD. And yeah, I mean there's a broad number of factors in it. Where we are focused currently is looking at, uh, as, um, Ray said, perioperative mortality, sweetie maternal mortality, uh, perinatal and maternal mortality.

And avoidable mortality with regard to cardiovascular disease, um, particularly regarding treatment for cardiovascular disease. Dr. Dr. Mathson, could you just repeat those? I got perioperative mortality, cardiovascular, sude, Perinatal maternal, yeah, sorry, peri perinatal and maternity.

Yeah, Perinatal and maternal. Thank You. Mm-hmm. And one thing, oh, sorry. And, And, um, one intervention that I recall from previous maternal and perinatal mortality review reports is improved access to maternal ultrasound. I mean, in terms of a single intervention that we're not doing that could be done, that would save some of these, um, avoidable, prevent some of these avoidable deaths.

Do you think that's significant, um, and uh, a helpful intervention? Or are there others you prioritize ahead of that? Uh, well, from what, from previous reports, we see it as a very helpful intervention, particularly if it reaches those, um, uh, those, uh, with the greatest need.

Um, so it's, uh, just two elements to it is, is their access, but then making sure that the people with the greatest need actually get access. One of the reasons for the shift that we've been making in the mortality review space, I know we've talked about this with you before, but is actually designed to try and shift the needle to get some of these recommendations implemented.

And so that's why we're quite excited about the perioperative stuff. 'cause it's actually, I mean, it sounds like it's just another group, but it's within Health New Zealand we're involved, there's a bunch of very senior relevant clinicians who are actually looking at what's happening with the mortality figures there. And we're hoping, you know, we can get some bit more real time action when we see things and identify them.

And how, how would you Oh, sorry. I was just going to say with regard to ultrasound, the, uh, the story over the mechanism was that the, um, mortality review Committee made it as a recommendation. Subsequent from that, the National Quality Forum took it up.

It took maternity and maternal health up as a issue of focus. Uh, and so, um, yeah, that, so that was sort of engaging the other sector director in it. Excellent. Thank, thank you. Um, another area of kind of doing, doing no harm or preventing preventing preventable harm is, um, hospital, uh, or healthcare associated infections.

Mm-hmm. Um, so what sort of programs have you been doing? What success have you had in that area? Um, Well ask Martin to come and join me, but my, we've had quite a significant program with various, you know, the, is the hospital acquired infections, hand washing, you know, hygiene and those kind of things. That program of work was one of the ones that got shifted back to, um, health New Zealand in June.

Um, but we have had some quite significant success with it over time. Martin is our expert in this area, So Martin Thomas, clinical director. Um, uh, yeah, as Ray alluded to the, the, um, infection prevention control program transition this year back to Health New Zealand. Um, I assume Meryl mentioned about, um, uh, other opportunities.

And one of the things that we were looking to work on, if the program had remained with us, was around peripheral iv, um, infections, um, and stuff, risk factor emus, which is, uh, a growing issue. So that would've would've been one of the other areas that we looked at. Um, healthcare associated infections, um, particularly postoperative infections. There is an opportunity to expand the work that we did in that, um, field, but we did it to take a, um, a point prevalence survey a number of years ago, which identified potential opportunities

to expand the healthcare associated infections, um, program. Uh, most of the healthcare effect associated infections are usually associated with, um, with implants, so peripheral IVs, ary tract, uh, sorry, ary catheters. So it's looking at, um, areas where you can produce packets and bundles of care to reduce those infections.

And we've had success previously with, um, joints, uh, so introducing, um, uh, programs through joint, joint joint reduced joint infections, um, which was very successful. Um, so Yeah, just looking to expand those, those Areas really, It's not, I mean it's not directly related, but it's probably relevant to mention that we have done quite a bit of work and continue to do in the sepsis area.

And, um, I was really pleased actually. It's good on a board. You often a little bit removed from what's going on, but I was at a function recently in a senior clinician or senior manager from Southern Cross Healthcare came up to me and said, thank you so much for those tools and for those resources for our staff. You know, they're really helping in terms of early identification and treatment of sepsis. So it's the kind of stuff we produce and people use.

Thank you. Okay, thank you. Just looking at, um, the sections around, um, enabling the workforce purpose in the survey and leading health quality intelligence, um, looking forward in terms of the survey that you've produced, how are you looking at incorporating, um, the patient's experience when it comes to staffing levels, say staffing levels more broadly and being able to also try to quantify that in the service that you do?

Some of that feedback, um, informs our insights reports that go up to the minister. So, um, ev everything that we do is informed from those patient surveys. So any opportunities we get through our work to provide information to decision makers, um, and to frontline services, uh, that it informs that it comes. And so what comes from the patients is used in that as well.

And so, particularly, you know, some of those insights reports do contain information around, uh, staffing levels and people's experience that there's a lot of, um, we get a lot of feedback, particularly around access to services from people Because across the health system we heard from nurses, alled, health doctors, et cetera, around concerns from safe staffing levels, from the insights we have produced so far.

What story does that tell you when it comes to, um, patients being able to access the health system where there are safe staffing levels? The story that we get from patients is that they still, um, and I'm looking at Richard, he can correct me if I get it wrong, but we still, um, hear from patients that they're waiting too long, but once they get into the system, we still consistently hear that people receive an amazing standard of care.

And, you know, with whatever's happening in terms of the pressures and challenges of staffing, it's clear that the staff themselves go, you know, above and beyond to ensure that people by and large get a very good experience. And finally, so in terms of the, um, programs you've done to improve the competency of workers when it comes to patient safety, which looks really, really good, do you think though that, um, there's concerns that the, if the efficacy of those programs maybe hindered by the fact that we are seeing in some wards

and in some, um, space in the health system, um, staff not being replaced when they leave and therefore having less staff being able to carry out the work that you're training them and equipping them to do? I I mean we just, we keep delivering the programs and they're still available. So I mean, people will come through as they, as they join up or as they, uh, come on board, people will undertake the training where it's available. I'm not quite sure.

So we have heard that, for example, a staff lead in some areas of the health system, they are not being currently replaced. If you have less, uh, staff being able to deliver the same amount of work in a shift, for example, um, do you not think that no matter how much training you give them, things will fall one way or the other, um, and go wayward as a result of lack of safe staffing levels?

It's a bit, it's a bit outside our, our remit in terms of safe staffing levels. I mean, what we see, if we see issues arising, whatever the cause relating to quality and safety, they'll come through the various avenues, um, including the adverse event reporting. And then I would, we would expect that the subsequent investigations by Health New Zealand or whoever the provider is, would then highlight whatever might have contributed to that event.

And if staffing's an issue, then that would be where it would be picked up. Thank you. Okay. We've got a few questions. We've got Cam, Jenny, I loss Asha, Thank you. This far, this is, uh, this is far from my area, um, but I'm asking about some of these, uh, work plan reporting tables that are in the manual report.

Uh, do you feel that the targets that are being fully achieved and delivered on are specific enough or, uh, you know, I'm, I'm reading them and it's coordinate the scoping phases with the, with the wider health sector to develop a strategy that defines the patient safety?

I mean, there's not, do you think that's quantifiable enough? This is the, what we struggle with, as I said to you, you know, when you're not directly, you know, like what we do and then the outcome, there's a whole lot of stuff that happens in between. Um, and so it's an ongoing challenge and you know, I've been on the board for a few years now. Every, uh, we are constantly grappling with how we can get better and clearer about how we measure what we do and how we report on it.

And we don't just support what we've done, but what we've achieved. So we've got a really good opportunity coming up because our statement of intent, um, is due for renewal, including these measures and the way in which our reporting, um, the reporting measures that we use. Um, and so we are meeting in February to talk about what our new strategic priorities are, what, um, it looks like and to, and what we are kind of, how we're gonna measure it.

And we will be continuing to try agree with you that, you know, they're really hard to kind of get here around sometimes and so we want to get clearer and we're working towards that, but noting the difficulties when you're a few steps back from Sure. What happens. Okay, well that's probably enough. Does that Answer your question? Yeah, That's good answer. Yeah. Okay, good. Um, thank you.

Just going back to mental health, um, you have a mandate which includes highlighting emergence, uh, emerging risks across services. We know that mental health is in crisis, particularly for youth work. There's a survey out currently by the mental health, um, uh, group in Auckland. We see increasing, uh, crisis need, um, worsening in equities and so on.

And we've seen, and, and we've had in this committee reference, uh, to a leader to minister ju from the health and safety Disability commissioner in relation to the tragic suicide of a 13-year-old. I've looked at a number of coroner's reports and um, the mental health commission itself has identified the lack of safety guards and rails and tracking of data with the 1 7, 3 7 and other telehealth services.

Why are you not monitoring that? And what would it take for your organization to formally identify these telehealth performance, um, services as a safety issue, Data gathering? Is that, yeah, I don't Think we got detail much. No. Yeah, I mean, I think, I mean, Ingrid, you know, I think there, there is an issue about avoiding duplication across the two commissions.

And so it would be a matter of needing to really clearly think about, um, what's, what is the bit that we can really add value to where we're not duplicating responsibilities or work being done by the commission, and then should we do it or should they do it? So I think, I think you're highlighting, highlighting an opportunity to do more. Um, uh, but I think that's about as much as I can say at this stage.

I, it sounds like from Richard, we don't actually have the necessarily data apart from if it pops up in other channels like mortality review or you know, those sorts of things. Patient experiences, Because we know commission, it's very much dependent on where they see a public interest or a need. Mm. Is this something that you will talk about in February when you talk about your strategic plan and the missing data and perhaps oversight for some of these other? We'll, 'cause you put it on my radar.

Thank you.
